sanskrit: अन्तवन्त इमे देहा नित्यस्योक्ताः शरीरिणः । अनाशिनोऽप्रमेयस्य तस्माद्युध्यस्व भारत ॥
hindiTranslation: अविनाशी, अप्रमेय और नित्य रहनेवाले इस शरीरी के ये देह अन्तवाले कहे गये हैं ।  इसलिये हे अर्जुन तुम युद्ध करो ॥
englishTranslation: These bodies are said to be finite, but the embodied being is eternal, indestructible, and immeasurable. Therefore, fight, Arjuna.
essence: What is truly you cannot be destroyed, so duty remains.
speaker: Krishna
simpleMeaning: Bodies are temporary, but the inner being is eternal and cannot be measured or destroyed. Arjuna is told to fight because of that truth.
simpleInsight: Fear loosens when you stop treating the body as the whole story.
detailedMeaning: On Kurukshetra, Arjuna is frozen before the war. Krishna answers his grief by separating the temporary body from the enduring embodied being. Because life is not destroyed by bodily change, Arjuna is told to do his duty and fight.
modernRelevance: You are standing outside a hospital room, about to make a hard decision for someone you love. The body can change, weaken, and end, but your duty does not vanish with your fear.
translationLiteral: अन्तवन्तः (ending / finite) / इमे (these) / देहाः (bodies) / नित्यस्य (of the eternal) / उक्ताः (are said to be) / शरीरिणः (of the embodied being) / अनाशिनः (of the indestructible) / अप्रमेयस्य (of the immeasurable) / तस्मात् (therefore) / युध्यस्व (fight) / भारत (Arjuna)
insight: It shifts the mind from bodily loss to rightful action.

quoteVariants.text: The body ends. The one within it does not. Fight.
Body ends. The bearer does not.
What wears out is the body, not the one within it.
